title: Quick check: Regions, AZs, and edge locations

questions.prompt: Why does deploying production workloads across multiple Availability Zones matter even within a single region?

choices.text: It doesn't matter — AZs are the same thing.

AZs are physically separate clusters of data centers within a region — miles apart, on different power grids, independent failure domains. Connected by fast low-latency networking (under 2ms) but designed so that one AZ failing (power outage, network issue, cooling failure) doesn't take down the others. A production workload running in a single AZ goes down when that AZ has issues. Deploying across 2-3 AZs (load balancer spanning AZs, app servers in multiple AZs, database with multi-AZ replication) means a single AZ failure leaves the system serving traffic from healthy AZs. This is the baseline durability pattern for production on AWS. Single-AZ deployments are appropriate for dev, test, or cost-sensitive non-critical workloads only. The cross-AZ data transfer cost is real but trivial compared to downtime cost.

questions.explanation: Multi-AZ is the durability baseline for production. AZs are independent failure domains by design. Skipping multi-AZ to save trivial transfer cost is a classic AWS mistake that produces outages.

questions.prompt: A team defaults to us-east-1 for everything because 'it's the cheapest region with the most services.' What's the concern?

choices.text: us-east-1 (Virginia) is the original AWS region, has the most services, and is typically the cheapest — but it's also the busiest region and has seen the most significant outages in AWS history (December 2021 multi-service outage being the most notable example). Many global AWS control-plane operations depend on us-east-1, so outages there can cascade beyond just us-east-1-hosted workloads. For non-critical workloads, the cost/service benefit may still win. For mission-critical workloads, consider us-east-2 (Ohio) or us-west-2 (Oregon) — comparable cost, similar service breadth, less concentrated impact during major incidents. The choice isn't 'never use us-east-1' but 'choose region with eyes open about exposure.' Teams running everything in us-east-1 should at least understand the risk and plan accordingly.

questions.explanation: us-east-1's history is part of its profile. Cheap and full-featured but the most-impacted by outages. The right region depends on workload criticality and the team's tolerance for concentrated exposure.
